subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Logical sequence from introduction through conclusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed (band 3) and well organized (band 3), but conciseness suffers (band 2) due to lengthy rawâdata tables and verbose passages. Overall, performance aligns most closely with a level-2 mark.

strengths: Generally consistent use of mathematical notation and terminology
Effective integration of graphs, tables and schematic diagrams
weaknesses: Inconsistent symbols (e.g. V_Î³ vs V_x) and occasional typographic slips
Some minimal captions and swapped variable definitions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are mostly consistent, multiple representations are used effectively, and the logical structure is clear with only minor ambiguities. This supports a solid band-3 performance.

strengths: Personal motivation (basketball free throws) clearly frames the investigation
Use of video analysis and selfâcollected data shows initiative
weaknesses: Approach closely mirrors classroom examples without novel extensions
Limited deeper reflection on how the project shaped personal understanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach, with good creativity, matching the descriptors for band 2.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on modelling limitations and errors
Thoughtful comparison of experimental and theoretical angles
weaknesses: Limited quantitative discussion of air resistance effects
Brief mention of future improvements without inâdepth evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is meaningful but not extensive, analysis is thoughtful yet not deeply critical, and evaluation of outcomes is clear but not comprehensive, consistent with band 2.

strengths: Relevant projectile-motion, regression and optimisation techniques applied effectively
Derivations and statistical summaries are generally correct and well presented
weaknesses: Omission of integration constant discussion and signâconvention clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is fully commensurate with the SL syllabus and is applied effectively, demonstrating good understanding with only minor errors, corresponding to band 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations accompany each derivation and graph, with logical step-by-step reasoning.  Minor ambiguities occur (e.g., swapping s_y and s_x descriptors), yet the narrative remains clear for a mathematically literate reader, satisfying the descriptor for good logical structure and clarity.

Derivations are generally correct and graphs fit well (rÂ²â0.99).  Minor notational errors and small mis-substitutions are present but do not undermine the main results; thus understanding is good and work is mostly accurate, meriting band 4 rather than 5 or 6.

The student provides tables, multiple time-series graphs, sââs_y plots and schematic diagrams.  These representations are integrated into the discussion and support the mathematics effectively, though some captions are minimal and a few axes are hard to read.  This aligns with âeffectiveâ (band 3) use of multiple representations.

The report follows a recognisable sequence â introduction, derivation of model, data collection, processing, optimisation, comparison and conclusion â so the reader can follow the argument without major difficulty.  Although some sentences are verbose and there are a few digressions, each mathematical step flows into the next and the purpose of each section is generally clear.  Overall coherence is therefore good, though not polished enough for âexcellentâ.

Using video analysis, regression and optimisation reflects good initiative, but the exploration does not introduce novel mathematical techniques or particularly creative extensions.  Hence âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ creativity and initiative are evident.

There is a thoughtful comparison of two methods and a brief consideration of rÂ² values and modelling limitations.  While insightful, the analysis does not probe deeply into sensitivity or error propagation, aligning with the âthoughtfulâ descriptor in band 2.

Outcomes are evaluated by comparing calculated optimal angles with literature values and observing sources of error.  Future improvements are noted only briefly, so the evaluation is clear but not comprehensive, satisfying band 2.

Assumptions are identified and later revisited; the student discusses how air resistance and release distance may explain the 4.7Â° discrepancy between model and data.  This constitutes meaningful but not extensive reflection, matching band 2.

Mathematical terms (trajectory, horizontal component, optimisation) and symbols (Î¸, Vâ, g) are generally used correctly, and equations are typeset clearly.  However, there are inconsistencies such as V_Î³, V_Î½ and occasional typographic slips, so usage is âmostlyâ rather than âfullyâ consistent.

The student frames the investigation around a personal interest in basketball and designs data collection personally, demonstrating a clear personal approach.  The approach, however, is not innovative enough for the highest band.

Most material is relevant to determining the optimum release angle, but the inclusion of full LoggerPro screenshots, repeated graphs and lengthy raw-data tables pads the essay to 32 pages and introduces repetition.  The work is therefore âmostlyâ relevant but not genuinely concise, matching band 2.

Projectile-motion equations, integration, regression analysis and trigonometric optimisation are all relevant and applied effectively to the problem.  The work goes beyond minimal requirements yet does not extend into higher-level mathematics (e.g., differential drag models), so the mathematics is ârelevant and used effectivelyâ rather than âcomprehensively/expertly.â

Collecting personal free-throw data, deciding on assumptions and deriving the model show noticeable independent thought beyond textbook exposition.  Nevertheless, the mathematical pathway (standard projectile motion) is well-known and closely mirrors classroom examples, so evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstanding.â

Headings, tables and figures are used consistently; there is a clear introduction and a brief conclusion, and the body is divided into logical sub-sections (kinematics, data tables, optimisation, etc.).  While rawâdata screenshots occupy several pages, the basic structural framework is clear and easy to navigate, fulfilling the descriptor for a âwell-organisedâ exploration.

All techniques lie comfortably within the AA SL syllabus (kinematics, basic calculus, regression).  Their application to real data is appropriate and shows fluency, fulfilling the criterion for mathematics âfully commensurate with course level.â

annotations.comment: The introduction is overly verbose and contains digressions. Consider streamlining by focusing on key motivations and removing repetitive phrases to enhance clarity and conciseness.
The assumption that air resistance is negligible is stated without discussing its quantitative effect. Reflect more deeply on how drag would alter the trajectory and optimal angle.
Defining the flat terrain assumption simplifies the model but undermines realism. Consider discussing how slight court inclines or floor compliance could influence results.
In the variables table, <math> s_y </math> and <math> s_x </math> definitions are swapped. Ensure <math> s_x </math> is horizontal displacement and <math> s_y </math> is vertical displacement.
Notation <math>V_{\gamma}</math> is used instead of <math>V_x</math>. Adopting consistent symbols throughout will avoid confusion in component decomposition.
The description âhorizontal component represents how fast the ball moves upwardâ is incorrect. Revise to state the horizontal component governs lateral motion.
The integration of gravity term omits a clear discussion of the constant of integration and sign conventions. Clarify how <math>c</math> corresponds to <math>h_0</math>.
Lines of gibberish (<math>\mathcal{L}</math>) appear in the derivation. Remove placeholders and ensure all displayed math is accurate and meaningful.
The clear derivation <math>t = \frac{s_x}{V_0\cos\theta}</math> demonstrates correct mathematical thinking in isolating time for substitution.
Including full Logger Pro data tables in the main text pads the discussion. Move extensive raw data to the appendix and summarize key values in the body.
Acknowledging loss of precision from rounding to three decimals shows good consideration of error sources.
Label âOptimizationâ clearly signals a new stage of the investigation. This structure enhances reader navigation.
Phrase âvery no variationâ in explaining <math>r^2</math> is unclear. Revise to more precise language when describing the coefficient of determination.
Caption for Figure 6 refers to <math>V_{\chi}</math> instead of <math>V_x</math>. Correct notation in captions to maintain mathematical consistency.
The unexpected high <math>V_y</math> value for trial 4 (5.804 m/s) is a clear outlier. Discuss why this anomaly occurred or consider excluding it when averaging.
Averaging <math>V_0</math> across all trials without addressing the outlier inflates the mean. Consider robust statistics (e.g., median) or justify inclusion.
Substitution in the optimization equation uses <math>s_x=3</math> despite earlier stating basket is 2 m away. Check consistency of parameter values.
Choosing the larger root (53.503Â°) for the optimal angle requires justification. Discuss physical reasoning for preferring the higher-angle solution.
Tabulated optimal angles and their average are clearly presented, reflecting good use of statistics to summarize results.
Comparison of model and experimental angles acknowledges a 4.7Â° discrepancy and links back to initial assumptions, demonstrating thoughtful critical analysis.
Conclusion effectively restates the aim and findings, linking to literature value (52Â°). Consider adding implications for training practice or further research.
References and bibliography are formatted consistently and include pertinent sources, demonstrating good research practice.
Personal interest in basketball is evident, but deeper reflection on how the project influenced your understanding could strengthen personal engagement.
Acknowledge how iterative model refinement (e.g., adding drag) could extend the exploration, offering avenues for further investigation.

similarExemplars.title: How does the angle of release of a basketball shot from the wrist analyzed using trigonometric principles, unit circle modeling, statistical data, parabolic trajectory analysis, and calculus support the hypothesis that close to 45 degrees angle is the most optimal for maximizing shot efficiency in terms of vertical height and horizontal displacement?
